content: Compare 3D Gaussian Splatting and Instant-NGP. Same task, same data — where does each win?

reasoning: Pull both pages, then numeric facts (PSNR/SSIM/FPS/train time), then neighbours for variants.
content: I'll pull both paper bodies, then search the temporal-fact layer for the shared metrics, then expand neighbours for the variants of each.

content.title: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering
content.body: Kerbl et al. introduce 3DGS: anisotropic 3D Gaussian primitives, differentiable tile rasteriser, SfM-seeded densification/pruning. >=30 FPS at 1080p on unbounded Mip-NeRF 360 scenes.

content.title: Instant Neural Graphics Primitives with a Multiresolution Hash Encoding
content.body: Mueller et al. introduce a multiresolution hash encoding that lets a small MLP fit radiance fields, SDFs, gigapixel images, and neural volumes. Fully-fused CUDA. Trains a high-quality radiance field in tens of seconds, renders at 1080p in tens of milliseconds.

reasoning: Compose the head-to-head matrix and close with one-paragraph guidance.
content:
Head-to-head on Mip-NeRF 360 (outdoor average):

  | Axis                          | 3D Gaussian Splatting (arxiv-2308-04079)   | Instant-NGP (arxiv-2201-05989)                |
  |-------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
  | PSNR                          | 27.21 dB                                   | 25.59 dB                                       |
  | SSIM                          | 0.815                                      | 0.671                                          |
  | FPS @ 1080p                   | >=134 (RTX A6000)                          | ~10 (RTX 3090)                                 |
  | Train time to converged       | ~41 min                                    | ~5 min (plateaus around 10 min)                |
  | Representation                | Anisotropic 3D Gaussians (explicit)        | Multi-resolution hash grid + tiny MLP (implicit)|
  | Task scope                    | Radiance field only; mesh via SuGaR/2DGS   | Radiance field, SDF, gigapixel image, volume   |
  | Memory at runtime             | Grows with primitive count (~1-4 GB)       | Fixed-size hash table (~50-500 MB)             |
  | Closest variants in corpus    | SuGaR, 2DGS, Scaffold-GS, Revising-Densification, MonoGS | Magic3D (text-to-3D backbone), MonoSDF (surface) |

**One-paragraph guidance.** 3DGS wins when the deliverable is a visibly-sharp novel-view render at interactive rates, the capture is a benign 100-image orbit, and you can spend 30-60 min of training per scene. Instant-NGP wins when you need (a) a task other than radiance field (it's the same code for SDF, gigapixel images, neural volumes), (b) faster initial convergence (5 minutes to usable quality vs 40 to converged), or (c) a fixed-memory footprint (the hash table size doesn't depend on scene complexity). The PSNR gap (1.6 dB) is real but smaller than the FPS gap (~13x); for offline rendering Instant-NGP is closer than the headline numbers suggest. For SLAM and mesh extraction, both have been superseded by their respective downstream variants (MonoGS for 3DGS, NeuS-on-hashgrid for Instant-NGP).
What about training memory? You listed train time and inference FPS but no VRAM number. That's the constraint that actually decides which method I can run.

reasoning: Both papers report training VRAM but in different units. 3DGS reports peak VRAM as a function of final Gaussian count; Instant-NGP reports a fixed hash-table footprint plus the small MLP. Let me search facts for both.
content: Both papers do report it but use different conventions. Pulling the numbers.

content.title: Revising Densification in Gaussian Splatting
content.body: Bulo et al. revise the clone/split criterion in 3DGS to a per-primitive variance-of-gradient measure; the revised schedule converges at ~70-80% of the original primitive count for matched PSNR, which translates to ~20-30% lower peak training VRAM on Mip-NeRF 360. The opacity-reset schedule is also recalibrated.

reasoning: Now I have a real VRAM column. The asymmetry is huge: Instant-NGP is ~3 GB peak regardless of scene; 3DGS scales with primitive count, peaks at 22 GB on the most complex Mip-NeRF 360 scenes. Bulo's revision shrinks that by 20-30%, taking the largest scenes to ~16-17 GB.
content:
Updated matrix with VRAM:

  | Axis                       | 3D Gaussian Splatting              | Instant-NGP                       |
  |----------------------------|------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
  | PSNR (MipNeRF360 outdoor)  | 27.21 dB                          | 25.59 dB                          |
  | SSIM                       | 0.815                             | 0.671                             |
  | FPS @ 1080p                | >=134 (RTX A6000)                 | ~10 (RTX 3090)                    |
  | Train time to converged    | ~41 min                           | ~5 min                            |
  | **Peak training VRAM**     | **10-22 GB (primitive-dependent)**| **~3 GB (fixed)**                 |
  | Inference VRAM             | 1-4 GB (final primitives only)    | ~0.5 GB (hash table)              |
  | Representation             | Anisotropic 3D Gaussians          | Multi-resolution hash grid + MLP  |
  | Task scope                 | Radiance field only               | Radiance field / SDF / image / volume |

The asymmetry is the headline. Instant-NGP's footprint is essentially constant — the hash table is fixed-size, the MLP is tiny — so it trains in ~3 GB regardless of scene. 3DGS scales linearly with the final primitive count: ~10 GB for a bicycle-scale scene, ~22 GB at the upper end of the Mip-NeRF 360 outdoor set (garden, treehill). Bulo's revised densification cuts 20-30% by holding the primitive count down. That still puts the worst scenes at ~16-17 GB.
On a 24GB consumer card — say a 4090 or a 3090 — which actually fits real scenes? Be specific. I want to know what scene size I can train without OOM.

reasoning: 24 GB minus driver/OS/CUDA overhead leaves ~22 GB usable. Instant-NGP fits trivially regardless of scene — 3 GB. 3DGS is right at the edge for the largest Mip-NeRF 360 outdoor scenes. The garden scene (the canonical hardest) trains at ~22 GB peak in vanilla 3DGS — that's exactly where OOM risk shows up on a consumer card if anything else is running. Let me search facts for scene-size statistics.
content: Math says Instant-NGP fits anything on 24 GB; 3DGS hits the wall on the largest Mip-NeRF 360 outdoor scenes. Let me check the specific scene-size stats to be precise.

arguments.query: Instant-NGP hash table size memory footprint

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-2201-05989
facts.object: Hash table footprint = 2^T * F * 4 bytes (T=19, F=2): ~4 MB per resolution level, 16 levels ~64 MB total

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-2201-05989
facts.object: Training overhead ~2.5 GB activations + ~0.1 GB MLP weights; total ~3 GB peak

reasoning: Concrete numbers: bicycle 19 GB, garden 22 GB. On a 24 GB card with ~2 GB driver/CUDA overhead, garden is right at the edge — practically you can train it but you can't have anything else on the card and the optimiser state needs to be checked. With Bulo's revision (~20-30% lower) garden drops to 15-17 GB, comfortable margin. Instant-NGP at 3 GB is irrelevant to the question — anything fits.
content:
Concrete recommendation for a 24 GB consumer card (4090 / 3090):

  **Instant-NGP — fits anything.** ~3 GB peak training regardless of scene complexity. The hash table is ~64 MB; the activations dominate at ~2.5 GB. Training a Mip-NeRF 360 outdoor scene leaves ~21 GB headroom for whatever else you're running. No scene-size limit you can practically hit on a single GPU.

  **3DGS (vanilla, Kerbl recipe) — fits everything Mip-NeRF 360 except the very largest outdoor scenes comfortably.**
    - Indoor scenes (room, counter, kitchen, bonsai): ~8-10 GB peak. Fits easily.
    - Mid outdoor (stump, treehill): ~15-18 GB peak. Fits with ~6 GB margin.
    - Largest outdoor (bicycle ~19 GB, garden ~22 GB): tight. The garden scene at 22 GB peak training leaves ~0-2 GB headroom on a 24 GB card after driver/CUDA overhead. Practically: you can train it but you can't share the card with any other workload, you should disable the desktop compositor, and a long training run risks OOM on a thermal spike that pushes a memory fragmentation event.

  **3DGS + Revised Densification (Bulo 2024) — fits everything comfortably.** The 20-30% primitive reduction takes garden from 22 GB to ~15-17 GB peak. Comfortable margin even on the hardest scenes. This is the recommended config for a 24 GB consumer card if you want to train Mip-NeRF 360 outdoor scenes without OOM anxiety.

  **What doesn't fit on 24 GB:** city-scale scenes (Block-NeRF / Mega-NeRF territory, 10M+ primitives) need a multi-GPU split or a more aggressive primitive-budget cap. Instant-NGP would still fit those in single-GPU training because the representation size is decoupled from scene complexity — but the fidelity at that scale is the trade-off that drove 3DGS's design in the first place.

So the concrete answer: on a 24 GB card, *both* methods fit *all* of the Mip-NeRF 360 benchmark scenes, but 3DGS-vanilla is at the OOM edge on the largest two outdoor scenes. Run with the Bulo revision if you want a margin. Instant-NGP is irrelevant to this constraint — it fits in any case.
